# TinyWeights.dev

> Small language models like Gemma, Phi, SmolLM, and Qwen, run locally. Benchmarks, quantization, and hands-on deployment guides for small LLMs on real hardware.

TinyWeights.dev is an independent blog focused exclusively on small language models (roughly 0.3B to 35B parameters) that run on local hardware: laptops, desktops, single-board computers, and phones. Coverage includes hands-on deployment guides, quantization explainers, benchmark comparisons, and hardware sizing. Posts cite official model cards, technical reports, and papers as primary sources, and include exact commands, measured file sizes, and RAM/VRAM requirements.

## Tools

- [Which model fits your hardware?](https://tinyweights.dev/fit/): Interactive wizard and VRAM/RAM calculator. Enter your memory and use case, get fitting models with recommended GGUF quantization.
- [Try a small LLM in your browser](https://tinyweights.dev/try/): Live WebGPU chat demo running quantized models (SmolLM2, Qwen2.5, Llama 3.2) entirely client-side via WebLLM.

## Posts

- [The Best Local Vision Language Models in 2026: Small VLMs You Can Actually Run](https://tinyweights.dev/posts/best-local-vision-language-models-2026/): A practical comparison of the best local vision language models in 2026, from 4B VLMs to 32B, with real MMMU and DocVQA benchmarks and VRAM estimates.
- [How to Run North Mini Code Locally: Cohere's 30B-A3B Coding Model](https://tinyweights.dev/posts/run-north-mini-code-locally/): Run North Mini Code 1.0 locally: Cohere's 30B-A3B open-weight coding MoE. GGUF sizes, the llama.cpp build for the cohere2moe architecture, hardware requirements, and benchmarks.
- [Local LLM for Private Document Q&A: Build an Offline RAG Pipeline](https://tinyweights.dev/posts/local-llm-private-document-qa/): How to build a fully private, offline document Q&A system with a local small LLM and an embedding model: exact Ollama commands, a runnable Python RAG script, and hardware requirements.
- [How to Quantize a Model with llama.cpp: From Safetensors to GGUF](https://tinyweights.dev/posts/how-to-quantize-model-llama-cpp/): Step-by-step guide to quantize a model with llama.cpp: convert HuggingFace safetensors to GGUF, run llama-quantize, and improve low-bit quants with an importance matrix.
- [DiffusionGemma: Google's Text-Diffusion Model That Generates 4x Faster, and How to Run It Locally](https://tinyweights.dev/posts/run-diffusiongemma-locally/): DiffusionGemma is Google's open-weight text-diffusion model: 25.2B total, 3.8B active, up to 4x faster generation than Gemma 4. How discrete text diffusion works, honest benchmarks, and how to run DiffusionGemma locally with vLLM, Transformers, and MLX.
- [How to Run LFM2.5-8B-A1B Locally: Liquid AI's On-Device Tool-Calling MoE](https://tinyweights.dev/posts/run-lfm2-5-8b-a1b-locally/): A practical guide to running LFM2.5-8B-A1B locally: Liquid AI's 8.3B mixture-of-experts activates only 1.5B parameters per token, fits in ~4.8GB at Q4, and ships with day-one Ollama, llama.cpp, and MLX support.
- [How to Run Phi-4-mini-reasoning Locally: Microsoft's 3.8B Math Model](https://tinyweights.dev/posts/run-phi-4-mini-reasoning-locally/): Step-by-step guide to running Phi-4-mini-reasoning locally with Ollama and llama.cpp: Microsoft's 3.8B model that outscores 7B distillation models on AIME and MATH-500.
- [How to Run Qwen3.6-35B-A3B Locally: A 35B MoE Model on One GPU](https://tinyweights.dev/posts/run-qwen3-6-35b-a3b-locally/): Run Qwen3.6-35B-A3B locally with Ollama, llama.cpp, or MLX. Hardware requirements, GGUF quantization sizes, and thinking-mode setup for the 35B model that activates only 3B parameters per token.
- [Run IBM Granite 4.1 Locally: 3B, 8B, and 30B Setup Guide](https://tinyweights.dev/posts/run-ibm-granite-4-1-locally/): How to run IBM Granite 4.1 locally with Ollama or llama.cpp. Full setup guide covering the 3B, 8B, and 30B models, hardware requirements, benchmark scores, and tool-calling.
- [Run ZAYA1-8B Locally: The 8B Reasoning MoE Trained on AMD](https://tinyweights.dev/posts/run-zaya1-8b-locally/): How to run ZAYA1-8B locally: a 8.4B-total, 760M-active reasoning MoE from Zyphra, with setup steps, memory requirements, and benchmark numbers.
- [1-bit LLMs Explained: How BitNet's Ternary Weights Actually Work](https://tinyweights.dev/posts/1-bit-llms-bitnet-ternary-weights/): A deep guide to 1-bit LLMs: how 1.58-bit ternary weights work, BitNet b1.58 2B4T benchmarks, running them on CPU with bitnet.cpp, and whether they beat 4-bit quantization.
- [How to Run LFM2.5-1.2B-Thinking Locally: On-Device Reasoning Under 1GB](https://tinyweights.dev/posts/run-lfm2-5-1-2b-thinking-locally/): A practical guide to running LFM2.5-1.2B-Thinking locally: Liquid AI's 1.2B reasoning model fits in under 1GB and runs on a phone CPU, with llama.cpp, LM Studio, and MLX support.
- [How to Run Jamba Reasoning 3B Locally: AI21's Hybrid SSM-Transformer With a 256K Context](https://tinyweights.dev/posts/run-jamba-reasoning-3b-locally/): A practical guide to running AI21's Jamba Reasoning 3B locally with Ollama and llama.cpp: a hybrid Mamba/Transformer 3B model with a 256K context window in under 2 GB at Q4.
- [GGUF Quantization Levels Explained: Q4, Q5, Q8, and IQ Quants](https://tinyweights.dev/posts/gguf-quantization-levels-q4-q5-q8/): What Q4_K_M, Q5_K_M, Q8_0, and the IQ-series quants actually cost you in file size, quality, and speed, with real benchmark numbers.
- [How to Run Ministral 3 Locally: Mistral's 3B, 8B, and 14B Vision Models](https://tinyweights.dev/posts/run-ministral-3-locally/): A practical guide to running Ministral-3 (3B, 8B, 14B) locally with Ollama and LM Studio: compact models with vision, tool use, and 256K context in under 10GB.
- [How to Run a Small LLM in Your Browser with WebLLM (No Install, No API)](https://tinyweights.dev/posts/run-llm-in-browser-webllm/): Run a small open LLM (Qwen2.5, SmolLM2, Llama-3.2) entirely in your browser using WebLLM and WebGPU. How it works, browser support, model sizes, and a live demo you can try right now.
- [Qwen3.5-4B vs Phi-4-mini: Choosing the Right 4B Model for Local Inference](https://tinyweights.dev/posts/qwen3-5-4b-vs-phi-4-mini/): Qwen3.5-4B vs Phi-4-mini: a direct comparison of benchmarks, VRAM, context windows, and multimodal support for the two best 4B local models in 2026.
- [MedGemma 1.5: Google's 4B Medical Vision-Language Model You Can Run Locally](https://tinyweights.dev/posts/medgemma-1-5/): MedGemma 1.5 is Google's 4B medical multimodal model: Gemma 3 backbone, MedSigLIP encoder, 128K context. Runs in 3.3GB on Ollama. Benchmarks, deployment, and the limits.
- [Running Local LLMs on Low-VRAM Windows GPUs (6GB and 8GB Cards) in 2026](https://tinyweights.dev/posts/run-local-llms-low-vram-windows-gpu/): Practical guide to running small language models on budget Windows GPUs with 6 GB or 8 GB of VRAM, with real token-speed benchmarks for RTX 3050, RTX 4060, and RTX 3060.
- [What Can You Actually Do With a Local Small LLM? A Practical Guide](https://tinyweights.dev/posts/what-can-you-do-with-local-small-llm/): A practical guide to real use cases for running small language models locally—from private document Q&A to offline coding assistants—with model and hardware recommendations for each.
- [Running LLMs on Raspberry Pi 5: A Practical Guide with Real Benchmarks](https://tinyweights.dev/posts/run-llms-raspberry-pi-5/): How to run small language models on Raspberry Pi 5 using Ollama and llama.cpp, with real token-speed benchmarks by model size and specific model recommendations for the 8 GB Pi.
- [The Complete Guide to Running Small LLMs on Apple Silicon (2026)](https://tinyweights.dev/posts/run-small-llms-apple-silicon/): Complete guide to running small language models on Apple Silicon — MLX vs Ollama, real benchmark data by chip generation, and setup commands for M1 through M5.
- [How to Run Phi-4-mini Locally: Microsoft's 3.8B Model with 128K Context](https://tinyweights.dev/posts/run-phi-4-mini-locally/): Step-by-step guide to running Phi-4-mini locally with Ollama, llama.cpp, and Python — with verified benchmarks and RAM requirements for Microsoft's 3.8B reasoning model.
- [How Much RAM Do You Actually Need to Run Local LLMs?](https://tinyweights.dev/posts/how-much-ram-for-local-llms/): Verified RAM and VRAM requirements for running local LLMs, with real Q4_K_M file size data for models from 1B to 32B parameters.
- [GGUF vs ONNX vs MLX: Which Model Format Should You Use for Local Inference?](https://tinyweights.dev/posts/gguf-vs-onnx-vs-mlx/): GGUF, ONNX, and MLX compared: what each format is for, which quantization levels to pick, and how to choose the right one for your hardware.
- [The Best Small Language Models in 2026: A Practical Comparison](https://tinyweights.dev/posts/best-small-language-models-2026/): A practical guide to the best small language models in 2026—Phi-4-mini, SmolLM3-3B, Gemma 3 4B, Qwen3, and more—with benchmarks, VRAM requirements, and honest use-case picks.
- [Ollama vs LM Studio vs llama.cpp: Which Local AI Runtime Should You Use?](https://tinyweights.dev/posts/ollama-vs-lm-studio-vs-llama-cpp/): Ollama vs LM Studio vs llama.cpp compared: setup, performance, API support, and which runtime to pick for your hardware and use case in 2026.
- [Qwen3.5-0.8B: A Multimodal Thinking Model That Fits in 1 Gigabyte](https://tinyweights.dev/posts/qwen3-5-tiny-multimodal-thinking-model/): Qwen3.5-0.8B packs multimodal vision, a 262K context window, and an on-demand thinking mode into a 1GB file. Here's the architecture behind it and how to run it locally.
- [Qwen3-Coder-Next: Run a Frontier-Level Coding Agent Locally on Consumer Hardware](https://tinyweights.dev/posts/qwen3-coder-next-local-coding-agent/): Alibaba's Qwen3-Coder-Next packs 80B total parameters into a 3B-active MoE, scores 70.6% on SWE-Bench Verified, and runs on a single RTX 5090 or 128GB Mac Studio.
- [Gemma 4: Taking Agentic Workflows to the Edge](https://tinyweights.dev/posts/gemma-4-taking-agentic-workflows-to-the-edge/): DeepMind's Gemma 4 brings 128K contexts, native 'thinking', and powerful multimodal agentic workflows to constrained devices and local hardware.
- [Deep Dive: Running Reka Edge Locally for Frontier-Level Vision AI on Mac and PC](https://tinyweights.dev/posts/run-reka-edge-locally-vision-model-mac/): An in-depth look at Reka Edge, the 7B multimodal vision-language model. Learn about its ConvNeXt V2 architecture, token efficiency, and how to run it locally on consumer hardware.
- [The 'Small' Model That Does It All: How Mistral Small 4's Unified Architecture Kills the Need for Specialized AI](https://tinyweights.dev/posts/the/): Discover how Mistral Small 4 unifies reasoning, multimodal, and coding into an efficient 119B MoE model that runs locally and slashes API costs.
- [AI in Your Pocket: How Liquid AI’s Apollo App Lets You Run Chatbots Completely Offline](https://tinyweights.dev/posts/ai-in-your-pocket-liquid-ai-apollo/): Discover how Liquid AI's Apollo app and LEAP platform bring fully private, offline small language models (SLMs) straight to your smartphone.
- [Out of the Cloud, Into the Wild: How Small AI Models and Physical AI Are Taking Over the Edge](https://tinyweights.dev/posts/out-of-the-cloud-into-the-wild/): Why the AI industry is pivoting from massive data centers to Small Language Models (SLMs) running locally on devices, factories, and 5G networks.
- [SmolLM3-3B: The Fully Open Small Language Model That Punches Way Above Its Weight](https://tinyweights.dev/posts/smollm3-3b-the-fully-ope/): SmolLM3-3B from HuggingFace is a fully open 3B SLM with 128k context, dual-mode reasoning, and multilingual support. Here's how to run it locally.
- [GPT-5.4 Nano: The Fastest, Cheapest OpenAI Model Yet — What Developers Need to Know](https://tinyweights.dev/posts/gpt-5-4-na/): GPT-5.4 nano is OpenAI's most efficient model yet — $0.20/M input tokens, 400k context, API-only. Here's what it can do and when to reach for it.
- [Fine-Tune Gemma 3 270M on Apple Silicon with MLX-LM and Python](https://tinyweights.dev/posts/fi/): A practical, step-by-step guide to fine-tuning Google's Gemma 3 270M on your Mac using Apple's MLX framework, LoRA, and Python.

## About

- [About](https://tinyweights.dev/about/): Who runs the site and what hardware the guides are tested on.
- [RSS feed](https://tinyweights.dev/index.xml): Full post feed.
